perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby undertakes to treat and maintain all Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties in confidence. With respect thereto, each party hereby undertakes and agrees as follows: <PAGE> i. For a period of 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party accept to those of its own employment having valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure shall be on terms not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby undertakes to treat and maintain all Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties in confidence. With respect thereto, each party hereby undertakes and agrees as follows: <PAGE> i. For a period of approximately 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall endeavor not to publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party except to those of its own employment having what they believe to be a valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure should be on terms that are generally not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear 5-year confidentiality period and prohibits disclosure except under specific, restrictive conditions. The modified text introduces ambiguity with 'approximately 5 years,' 'endeavor not to publish,' and 'what they believe to be a valid need to know.' This weakens the obligation, potentially contradicting California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, using subjective terms like 'approximately' and 'endeavor', making it harder to prove a violation of trade secret protection under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Specifically, the original text states 'For a period of 5 years', while the changed text says 'For a period of approximately 5 years'. Furthermore, 'endeavor not to publish' is used instead of 'shall not publish'. These changes introduce uncertainty and discretion where the original text was more definitive. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change contradicts California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1, from California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the confidentiality obligations with ambiguous language, the modified contract makes it more difficult to prove a breach of that duty, creating a legal contradiction because the original text provided a clear, enforceable standard that is now undermined.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information as it uses in respect of its own information of like importance but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall use a degree of care it deems appropriate to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a baseline for the degree of care required to protect confidential information, referencing the receiving party's own information and setting a 'reasonable degree of care' as the minimum. The modified text replaces this with a subjective standard of 'a degree of care it deems appropriate.' This introduces significant ambiguity and weakens the protection of the confidential information. This can contradict California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) standard of 'reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Giving the receiving party sole discretion to determine the 'degree of care' weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets and undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required under CUTSA, making it more difficult to enforce confidentiality.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts CUTSA by weakening the requirement to protect trade secrets. The original text mandates 'a reasonable degree of care', while the changed text allows the receiving party to use 'a degree of care it deems appropriate.' This subjective standard undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required under CUTSA, as articulated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' under CIV 3426.1, which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, highlighting the legal importance of protecting trade secrets, something diminished by the new contractual term.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that necessary authorizations, permits or licenses including expert licenses as may be required will be obtained prior to the exportation/disclosure of any Proprietary/Confidential Information relating to the technology of the other party. The disclosing party shall notify the receiving party of the need to obtain any required authorizations, permits and licenses and/or the need to comply with any relevant laws or regulations relating to the disclosure. The disclosing party shall obtain the required authorizations, permits and licenses.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that authorizations, permits or licenses may be obtained prior to the exportation/disclosure of any Proprietary/Confidential Information relating to the technology of the other party. The disclosing party may notify the receiving party of the need to obtain authorizations, permits and licenses and/or comply with relevant laws or regulations relating to the disclosure. The disclosing party will endeavor to obtain the required authorizations, permits and licenses.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that necessary authorizations *will* be obtained. The modified text makes obtaining authorizations, permits, and licenses discretionary by replacing 'will' with 'may' and 'shall' with 'endeavor to'. Export regulations, particularly those concerning technology, are strict. This introduces ambiguity about who is responsible for ensuring compliance with export regulations, potentially contradicting federal law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR require strict adherence to export control laws. By making the process of obtaining necessary authorizations ambiguous and not mandatory, the modified text creates a situation where a party might inadvertently violate export laws due to the unclear responsibilities, contradicting the strict compliance mandated by the EAR.

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the mandatory nature of obtaining necessary authorizations, potentially leading to non-compliance with export regulations. The original text clearly mandates that authorizations 'will' be obtained, whereas the changed text makes it discretionary by stating authorizations 'may' be obtained and replacing 'shall' with 'endeavor to'. This shift contradicts the strict compliance typically associated with export laws, as highlighted by the Law Explanation concerning the EAR (Export Administration Regulations). While the provided law snippets don't explicitly state the word "mandatory", the emphasis on 'Steps for Using the EAR' in snippet 1, and the overall framework of the EAR as detailed in snippet 2, implies a structured and controlled process, making the discretionary language in the modified text a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies the weakening of the obligation, making it unclear who is responsible for ensuring compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the disclosing party of such Proprietary/Confidential Information orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the disclosing party agrees to promptly notify the receiving party of the confidentiality of such oral disclosure and reduces to writing such Proprietary/Confidential Information and submit the same to the receiving party within 15 days of such oral disclosure, upon which the receiving party shall not be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the disclosing party of such Proprietary/Confidential Information orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the disclosing party should consider notifying the receiving party of the confidentiality of such oral disclosure and may reduce to writing such Proprietary/Confidential Information and submit the same to the receiving party within approximately 15 days of such oral disclosure, upon which the receiving party shall not necessarily be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear procedure for oral disclosures, requiring the disclosing party to notify the receiving party and provide written documentation within 15 days to maintain confidentiality. The modified text weakens this by replacing 'agrees to' with 'should consider', 'may reduce to writing' instead of a definitive instruction, and adding 'approximately 15 days' which introduces ambiguity. Finally, 'shall not necessarily be bound' undermines the entire point of the clause. This can contradict the reasonable efforts requirement under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text creates uncertainty and diminishes the disclosing party's responsibility to properly document and protect oral disclosures, weakening the overall protection and making it harder to claim trade secret misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the disclosing party's obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy under CUTSA. The original text's definitive terms ('agrees to', 'reduces to writing', 'shall not be bound') are replaced with weaker, discretionary language ('should consider', 'may reduce to writing', 'shall not necessarily be bound'). This shift undermines the protection of trade secrets. Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. The weakened language in the modified contract makes it more difficult to prove such a breach, increasing the risk of misappropriation.
